(20 August 1879 – 23 July 1957) was an Italian general during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). In 1939, he assumed command of the Italian forces in [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"). In 1940, he served as commander of the [11th Army](/wiki/Eleventh_Army_(Italy) "Eleventh Army (Italy)") during the [Greco-Italian War](/wiki/Greco-Italian_War "Greco-Italian War"). He was the commander of the Italian [occupation forces](/wiki/Axis_occupation_of_Greece "Axis occupation of Greece") in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") from 1941 to 1943. After [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") joined the Allies, he became a German [prisoner-of-war](/wiki/Prisoner-of-war "Prisoner-of-war"). After the German surrender, he was briefly imprisoned by the [Soviets](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") but was released in 1946.

**Carlo Geloso** (20 August 1879 – 23 July 1957) was an Italian general during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). In 1939, he assumed command of the Italian forces in [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"). In 1940, he served as commander of the [11th Army](/wiki/Eleventh_Army_(Italy) "Eleventh Army (Italy)") during the [Greco-Italian War](/wiki/Greco-Italian_War "Greco-Italian War"). He was the commander of the Italian [occupation forces](/wiki/Axis_occupation_of_Greece "Axis occupation of Greece") in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") from 1941 to 1943. After [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") joined the Allies, he became a German [prisoner-of-war](/wiki/Prisoner-of-war "Prisoner-of-war"). After the German surrender, he was briefly imprisoned by the [Soviets](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") but was released in 1946.

## Early life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Geloso&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and career")]

Born in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") on 20 August 1879 to Bonaventura Geloso and Fortunata Burgio di Villanova,[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1) Carlo Geloso entered the Military Academy of Artillery and Engineers of [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") on 27 October 1898. After graduation, he was commissioned as a second lieutenant of artillery on 26 August 1901 and continued his studies at the Application School of Artillery and Engineers at Turin. He graduated in 1903 and being promoted to lieutenant on 11 August.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010396-2) He was assigned to the 3rd Fortress Artillery Regiment. On 28 December 1907 he wed Angela Rocaglia, and in April 1909, he was transferred to the 15th Field Artillery Regiment. He successfully completed the general staff course in Turin in 1910, and was promoted to captain by selection (before the regular promotion period) on 30 November 1911. Until 1912, he served in the staffs the Territorial Military Divisions of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and then [Napoli](/wiki/Napoli "Napoli").[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010396-2) In July 1912, he embarked for [Tripolitania](/wiki/Italian_Tripolitania "Italian Tripolitania"), to establish a commissariat for the Italian expeditionary corps at Zuara. He returned to Italy on 5 August, but he remained in command of the commissariat until April 1914, when he was appointed to the 9th Fortress Artillery Regiment.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)

## World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Geloso&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: World War I")]

After Italy entered [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), he was sent to serve in a staff position in the [Julian Alps](/wiki/Julian_Alps "Julian Alps") Front. On 9 November 1915, he was promoted to major.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010396-2) In May 1916, he was appointed chief of staff of the [31st Infantry Division](/w/index.php?title=31st_Infantry_Division_(Italy)&action=edit&redlink=1 "31st Infantry Division (Italy) (page does not exist)"). His participation in the [Sixth Battle of the Isonzo](/wiki/Sixth_Battle_of_the_Isonzo "Sixth Battle of the Isonzo") with the division earned him his first [Silver Medal of Military Valor](/wiki/Silver_Medal_of_Military_Valor "Silver Medal of Military Valor").[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1) In February 1917, he was transferred to the general staff. In May he won his second Silver Medal of Military Valor for his participation in the [Tenth Battle of the Isonzo](/wiki/Tenth_Battle_of_the_Isonzo "Tenth Battle of the Isonzo") in the Bosco Malo-Pod Horite area and was promoted to lieutenant-colonel on 31 May.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1) As chief of staff of the [65th Infantry Division](/w/index.php?title=65th_Infantry_Division_(Italy)&action=edit&redlink=1 "65th Infantry Division (Italy) (page does not exist)") from July, he fought in the [Eleventh Battle of the Isonzo](/wiki/Eleventh_Battle_of_the_Isonzo "Eleventh Battle of the Isonzo") in the [Mešnjak](/w/index.php?title=Me%C5%A1njak&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mešnjak (page does not exist)") area and won his third Silver Medal in the process, and then, he fought in the disastrous [Battle of Caporetto](/wiki/Battle_of_Caporetto "Battle of Caporetto").[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1) From April 1918 he was chief of staff of the [34th Infantry Division](/w/index.php?title=34th_Infantry_Division_(Italy)&action=edit&redlink=1 "34th Infantry Division (Italy) (page does not exist)"). His organizational ability was recognised in September by a commendation and the award of a knighthood in the [Military Order of Savoy](/wiki/Military_Order_of_Savoy "Military Order of Savoy").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010396–397-3) After the armistice, he spent some time in the [Tonale](/wiki/Monte_Tonale "Monte Tonale")–[Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda") area, moved to the post of commandant in [Padua](/wiki/Padua "Padua") and then joined the Rome garrison and the general staff.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)

## Interwar period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Geloso&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Interwar period")]

Despite his distinguished if unexceptional career, from 20 July 1920 to 7 March 1925 he was relegated to a "Special Auxiliary Position", a special arrangement intended to handle the large number of officers after the end of the war.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010397-4) During that time, he became a member of the [Italian Fascist Party](/wiki/Italian_Fascist_Party "Italian Fascist Party") on 4 May 1921 and was promoted to colonel on 31 December 1924.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)

On his return to active duty in 1925, he was assigned to the General Secretariat of the Supreme Commission on Defence, and he left the service from February 1926.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010397-4) He remained at that post until 1 March 1928, when he was sent to command the 6th Heavy Field Artillery Regiment.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010397-4)

On 25 January 1931 he was promoted brigadier general, and in March 1931, he returned to Rome as chief of staff to the [VIII Army Corps](/wiki/VIII_Army_Corps_(Italy) "VIII Army Corps (Italy)"). He then served as artillery commander of the [III Army Corps](/wiki/III_Army_Corps_(Italy) "III Army Corps (Italy)") at [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") in 1933, before being appointed acting chief of staff for the [X Army Corps](/wiki/X_Army_Corps_(Italy) "X Army Corps (Italy)") at [Napoli](/wiki/Napoli "Napoli") in 1934 and for the [VI Army Corps](/wiki/VI_Army_Corps_(Italy) "VI Army Corps (Italy)") at [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna") in 1935.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010398-5) On 25 November 1935, he was appointed to the highly-prestigious and sought-after post of commander of the [*Granatieri di Sardegna* Infantry Division](/wiki/21st_Infantry_Division_Granatieri_di_Sardegna "21st Infantry Division Granatieri di Sardegna") in Rome.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010398-5)

### Ethiopia and Albania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Geloso&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ethiopia and Albania")]

In March 1936 he was sent to [Italian Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland") to command the Special Infantry Division "Laghi" during the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War"). There, he occupied the area of the equatorial area and defeated the Ethiopian *[ras](/wiki/Ras_(title) "Ras (title)")* [Desta Damtew](/wiki/Desta_Damtew "Desta Damtew"). In October 1936 he became governor of the area, now the [Galla-Sidamo Governorate](/wiki/Galla-Sidamo_Governorate "Galla-Sidamo Governorate"), as well as commander of the local Italian garrison. For his service, he was promoted to divisional general in December and received a further Military Order of Savoy. He remained at that position until 1938.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010398–399-6) His violent suppression of [Ethiopian resistance](/wiki/Arbegnoch "Arbegnoch"), particularly in the wake of a failed assassination attempt on Viceroy [Rodolfo Graziani](/wiki/Rodolfo_Graziani "Rodolfo Graziani") in February 1937, led to him being listed as a [war criminal](/wiki/War_criminal "War criminal") by the Ethiopian government, which attempted to have him extradited to Ethiopia after the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War").[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)

In 1938, he returned to Rome, until 3 August as Honorary Governor of Galla-Sidamo, but in 1939, he was promoted to army corps general and sent to command the Bari Army Corps. Promoted to army corps general, he was sent in October to command first the [VII Army Corps](/wiki/VII_Army_Corps_(Italy) "VII Army Corps (Italy)") at [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") and then, on 1 December, the [XXVI Army Corps](/wiki/XXVI_Army_Corps_(Italy) "XXVI Army Corps (Italy)"), comprising the Italian garrison ([XXVI Army Corps](/wiki/XXVI_Army_Corps_(Italy) "XXVI Army Corps (Italy)")) in [Italian-occupied Albania](/wiki/Italian_protectorate_of_Albania_(1939%E2%80%931943) "Italian protectorate of Albania (1939–1943)").[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010399-7)

Geloso remained in Albania until 6 June 1940. During his tenure, he planned for an attack on Greece, and elements of his plans were indeed used in the subsequent [Italian invasion of Greece](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Greece "Italian invasion of Greece"), but it was under quite different conditions and participating forces from his plans. Although a capable organiser, he was helpless in the backroom political manoeuvring that characterized the Fascist regime. After disagreeing with Foreign Minister [Galeazzo Ciano](/wiki/Galeazzo_Ciano "Galeazzo Ciano"), [Mussolini](/wiki/Mussolini "Mussolini")'s heir-apparent, during a discussion of a possible war with Greece on 23 April 1941, he was replaced by [Sebastiano Visconti Prasca](/wiki/Sebastiano_Visconti_Prasca "Sebastiano Visconti Prasca").[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)

## World War II and occupation of Greece

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Geloso&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: World War II and occupation of Greece")]

Italy's entry into [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") found him as head of the [Third Army](/wiki/Third_Army_(Italy) "Third Army (Italy)"), but he returned to Albania on 15 November 1940 to take up command of the newly formed [Eleventh Army](/wiki/Eleventh_Army_(Italy) "Eleventh Army (Italy)") in the ongoing war against Greece.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010399-7) After the [German invasion of Greece](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Greece "German invasion of Greece") and the start of the [country's occupation](/wiki/Axis_occupation_of_Greece "Axis occupation of Greece"), in September 1941 he was named Supreme Commander of the Italian Armed Forces in Greece, while remaining commander of Eleventh Army. He kept both positions until his dismissal in May 1943. In October 1942, he was promoted to army general.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010399-7)

His tenure in Greece earned him criticism from many sides. The Greeks naturally resented him as the commander-in-chief of the Italian occupation forces and the brutality that he displayed in anti-partisan operations. However, the Italians' German allies considered him too "weak" and ineffective despite his reputation as a loyal adherent to the [Axis alliance](/wiki/Axis_Powers "Axis Powers"). Geloso opposed the Germans' demands for even harsher retaliations and even resisted the demands for implementation of discriminatory measures against Greek Jews in the Italian occupation zone.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1) On the other hand, Geloso in spring 1943 approved the institution of [collective punishment](/wiki/Collective_punishment "Collective punishment") against the civilian population in retaliation for partisan attacks, including measures such as "aerial bombardment and heavy artillery fire", the "pillaging of their food supplies", and "the deportation to concentration camps of the village chiefs and all of the men who made up the community council". That order led to a series of atrocities over the following months, such as the [Domenikon massacre](/wiki/Domenikon_massacre "Domenikon massacre").[[8]](#cite_note-Santarelli-8)

At the same time, Geloso's relations with the Germans became increasingly strained, as he jealously guarded the Italian prerogatives in Greece against German encroachments, particularly after the appointment of Colonel-General [Alexander Löhr](/wiki/Alexander_L%C3%B6hr "Alexander Löhr") as the German Commander-in-chief Southeast in December 1942. The appointment came as a result of the worsening situation on the North African Front, which made an Allied landing in Greece increasingly likely. The Germans, who did not trust the Italians' ability to withstand such an attack, began moving more of their own troops into Greece, sought to supplant the Italians in certain strategic areas including Athens and the [Athens–Thessaloniki railway](/wiki/Athens%E2%80%93Thessaloniki_railway "Athens–Thessaloniki railway") and justified the move by thereby making additional Italian troops available to defend western Greece. Geloso vehemently refused to consider that and, in a later meeting, counter proposed for any additional German forces in Greece to be placed under his command. With the backing of the [Italian high command](/wiki/Comando_Supremo "Comando Supremo"), the situation remained unresolved, with Geloso even proposing setting up an "Army Group East" under his command, comprising all Italian forces in the occupied areas of the Balkans, as a counterpart bans counterweight to Löhr's command, with himself at its head.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010319–333-9)

Despite being highly praised by the chief of the Italian General Staff, [Ugo Cavallero](/wiki/Ugo_Cavallero "Ugo Cavallero"), as "perhaps the best of our generals, technically prepared, energetic and tactful", Geloso was dismissed on 3 May and placed under the disposition of the Ministry of War. The likely reason was his final disagreement with Löhr in a meeting a few days earlier. Löhr demanded from the Italians a series of measures to curb the growing [Greek Resistance](/wiki/Greek_Resistance "Greek Resistance"), including the arrest of the officers of the Greek army, the confiscation of all radios and the handover of all Jews in the Italian zone to German control.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1) An Italian investigation, headed by Admiral [Domenico Cavagnari](/wiki/Domenico_Cavagnari "Domenico Cavagnari"), into his tenure in Greece ended up absolving him of all charges brought against him for malpractice and corruption.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin20103,_400-10)

## Imprisonment and release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Geloso&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Imprisonment and release")]

On 20 June 1943, he was placed in the reserve because of his advanced age but was recalled to service attached to the Ministry of War. In the days before the [Italian armistice](/wiki/Italian_armistice "Italian armistice"), he was placed in command of the troops intended to defend Rome, but he never actually exercised a field command. He was captured by the Germans on 23 September and sent to an [officers' camp](/wiki/Oflag "Oflag") at [Schokken](/wiki/Schokken "Schokken"). He was liberated by the arrival of [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") troops, sent to [Kharkov](/wiki/Kharkov "Kharkov"), and thence returned to Italy on 9 October 1945.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010400-11) During his imprisonment, Geloso wrote to Graziani, the Minister of War in the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") (RSI), asking for his intervention to secure his release; but, appeared not to have joined the RSI.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010401-12) On his return to Italy, he was asked to compile a series of memoranda on his fellow prisoners and their behaviour in captivity.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010401-12)

## Later life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Geloso&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Later life")]

He was placed on leave on 1 February 1946 but was recalled to temporary service from 27 April to 30 June 1947. On 28 May 1947, the Italian monarchy [having been abolished the previous year](/wiki/Italian_institutional_referendum,_1946 "Italian institutional referendum, 1946") he swore an oath of loyalty to the new [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic "Italian Republic"). On 1 May 1954, he was placed in permanent leave because of his advanced age. He died at Rome on 23 July 1957.[[1]](#cite_note-Massignani2000-1)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregantin2010401-12)
